question: Is the NuScale integral pressurizer design adequate for managing RCS pressure transients?

nuscale_ch05_0019.title: Reactor Coolant System Component and Subsystem Design
nuscale_ch05_0019.summary: This section describes the design of the NuScale Power Module’s Reactor Coolant System (RCS) components and subsystems, including the reactor pressure vessel, steam generators, safety and emergency valves, and associated piping. It details how the steam generators are integrated into the reactor vessel, forming part of the primary and secondary pressure boundaries, and references other sections for specific component design bases.

nuscale_ch05_0023.title: Reactor Coolant System High-Point Vents
nuscale_ch05_0023.summary: This section describes the testing and inspection requirements for the Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) components in the NuScale reactor, ensuring compliance with ASME codes for Class 2 components. It details how DHRS actuation valves are tested during cold shutdown conditions, outlines the procedures for valve exercising and position verification, and specifies that a one-time in-situ test of the DHRS function will be performed on the first reactor module during initial startup.

nuscale_ch05_0004.title: System Description
nuscale_ch05_0004.summary: The Reactor Coolant System (RCS) in the NuScale design is located inside the containment vessel and includes components such as the reactor pressure vessel, pressurizer, and associated piping and controls. It removes heat from the reactor core to the steam generators using natural circulation, and features systems for removing noncondensible gases and maintaining pressure boundary integrity to prevent radionuclide release. The RCS interfaces with several other systems, including emergency core cooling and chemical control, to ensure safe and efficient operation.

nuscale_ch05_0022.title: Decay Heat Removal System
nuscale_ch05_0022.summary: The Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) is an engineered safety feature designed to remove residual and core decay heat from the reactor after shutdown, especially when normal cooling is unavailable, without relying on external power. It ensures the reactor coolant system temperature drops below 420°F within 36 hours, maintaining fuel and pressure boundary safety limits, and its reliability and risk significance are rigorously evaluated. The DHRS operates independently of the Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS), but both can function together if needed.

nuscale_ch05_0024.title: Pressurizer
nuscale_ch05_0024.summary: The pressurizer, integrated into the upper region of the reactor vessel and separated by a baffle plate, regulates Reactor Coolant System (RCS) pressure by maintaining a saturated steam-water interface. It uses heaters and spray flow to control pressure and is designed to handle operating transients without triggering reactor trips or safety system actuations, with instrumentation providing continuous monitoring and control.

nuscale_ch05_0012.title: Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary Leakage Detection
nuscale_ch05_0012.summary: The NuScale reactor coolant pressure boundary (RCPB) leakage detection system does not use traditional components with designed leakage rates, making it impractical to distinguish between identified and unidentified leakage inside containment using installed instrumentation. All leakage into containment is treated as unidentified until the source is located, with leakage rates determined by RCS inventory balance and compared to the containment evacuation system (CES) flow; the detection systems are reliable and support leak-before-break analyses.

nuscale_ch05_0009.title: Overpressure Protection
nuscale_ch05_0009.summary: This section outlines NuScale's compliance with NRC and ASME code requirements for overpressure protection of the reactor coolant boundary. It details the use of specific ASME BPVC and OM code cases for design, fabrication, construction, preservice, and inservice inspections and testing, ensuring all selected code cases meet NRC regulatory guidance and conditions. A consolidated list of these code cases is provided in Table 5.2-1.

nuscale_ch05_0008.title: Compliance with Codes and Code Cases
nuscale_ch05_0008.summary: This section describes how the NuScale Power Module (NPM) and its reactor coolant pressure boundary (RCPB) components comply with relevant U.S. regulations, specifically 10 CFR 50.55a and associated General Design Criteria. It details the use of ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code (BPVC) standards for design, fabrication, and testing, specifying quality classifications and the applicable code editions, ensuring regulatory and safety requirements are met.

nuscale_ch05_0018.title: References
nuscale_ch05_0018.summary: This section outlines the inspection, shipment, installation, and surveillance requirements for the NuScale reactor vessel, referencing relevant ASME and ASTM standards for quality assurance and material integrity. It specifies procedures for maintaining a dry, controlled environment during shipment and details where further information on inspections, operating conditions, and fasteners can be found in the report. Key industry codes and standards used are also listed as references.

nuscale_ch05_0002.title: Summary Description
nuscale_ch05_0002.summary: The NuScale reactor coolant system (RCS) uses natural circulation, eliminating the need for pumps or external piping, and is integrated within each NuScale Power Module (NPM). The RCS is designed to safely transfer heat from the reactor core, maintain proper coolant chemistry, and ensure uniform boron concentration, supporting both normal and off-normal plant conditions. These features collectively ensure the safety and performance of each NPM in the NuScale Power Plant.

nuscale_ch05_0003.title: Design Basis
nuscale_ch05_0003.summary: The Design Basis section outlines that the NuScale reactor coolant system (RCS) uses natural circulation without pumps or external piping, ensuring safe and efficient heat transfer from the reactor core to the steam generator. The RCS is designed to maintain proper coolant flow, water chemistry, and boron concentration to protect fuel integrity and support safe operation under normal and transient conditions.

nuscale_ch05_0006.title: System Evaluation
nuscale_ch05_0006.summary: The System Evaluation section describes how the NuScale Power Plant optimizes performance across various power levels by establishing steady-state parameters such as coolant temperatures, flow rates, and pressures, using both software and manual calculations. The evaluation considers uncertainties and design requirements to ensure efficient operation, maximize electrical generation, and maintain safety margins, with a focus on supporting natural circulation and minimizing pressure losses in the reactor coolant system.

edges.description: Section A describes the overall design and components of the NuScale reactor coolant system, including the pressurizer and high-point vents, while Section B specifies the inspection and classification requirements for high-point vent components, connecting them through the regulatory compliance of subsystem components mentioned in Section A.

Section A describes the physical integration of the pressurizer within the NuScale Power Module's reactor pressure vessel, while Section B explains the operational features of the pressurizer, such as degasification and pressure control, both contributing information relevant to assessing the adequacy of the pressurizer design for managing RCS pressure transients.

Section A lists the decay heat removal system (DHRS) as a subsystem within the reactor coolant system (RCS) design, while Section B provides the design basis and functional description of the DHRS, thereby specifying details for the subsystem mentioned in Section A.

Section A provides a general overview of the NuScale reactor coolant system components, including the integral pressurizer, while Section B specifies the detailed design and function of the pressurizer as part of that system.

Section A outlines inspection and testing requirements for RCS components, including valves, while Section B describes the function of reactor vent valves in managing pressurizer gases, connecting them through the operational and regulatory oversight of components critical to RCS pressure management.

Section A describes inspection and classification requirements for DHRS components, which are part of the system described in Section B as providing decay heat removal and RCS cooling during transients, thus Section A provides specific implementation details for the general DHRS design basis in Section B.

Section A describes the pressurizer's role in managing RCS pressure and gas removal, while Section B outlines the decay heat removal system's function in cooling the RCS during transients, with both sections connected by their roles in maintaining RCS pressure and integrity during abnormal conditions.

Section A provides a general system-level description of how the RCS and pressurizer manage gases and pressure, while Section B specifies the detailed design and functional integration of the pressurizer within the reactor vessel, including its role in pressure communication.

Section B describes the integral pressurizer's design and its function to rapidly communicate pressure changes within the RCS, which is relevant to Section A's discussion of the DHRS's ability to manage RCS pressure transients during non-LOCA events.

Section A discusses the limitations of leakage detection in the NuScale RCS, while Section B outlines the components and subsystems—including the integral pressurizer—whose design must account for such operational challenges, connecting them through the need for adequate design to manage pressure and leakage.

Section A discusses the challenges in detecting RCS boundary leakage due to the NuScale design, while Section B outlines inspection and testing requirements for RCS components, both addressing aspects of system integrity relevant to evaluating the pressurizer's adequacy in managing pressure transients.

Section A discusses the challenges in detecting RCS boundary leakage due to instrumentation limitations, while Section B describes the RCS design features (such as degasification and venting) that help maintain RCPB integrity, thus Section B's design features support the adequacy of the pressurizer design referenced in Section A's context.

Section A references regulatory requirements and code compliance for overpressure protection, while Section B describes the physical components of the RCS, including the integral pressurizer, thus Section B provides the specific system design that must comply with the requirements outlined in Section A.

Section A outlines compliance with ASME BPVC and regulatory requirements for overpressure protection, while Section B details inspection and classification requirements for RCS components, both referencing the application of ASME BPVC standards to ensure system integrity.

Section A outlines the regulatory codes and standards (ASME BPVC, 10 CFR 50.55a) governing the design and construction of the reactor coolant boundary, while Section B describes the physical features and functions of the RCS and pressurizer that must comply with those codes.

Section A states that the NuScale pressurizer and associated RCS components comply with ASME BPVC Class 1 requirements, while Section B describes the specific components included in the RCS design, including the integral pressurizer, thus Section B provides the detailed system context for the compliance claim made in Section A.

Section A establishes that the NuScale reactor coolant pressure boundary components, including the pressurizer, comply with ASME BPVC Section III Class 1 requirements, while Section B details that related components (such as DHRS actuation valves) meet ASME BPVC Section XI and OM Code inspection and testing requirements, together demonstrating adherence to regulatory codes for pressure boundary integrity and operational reliability relevant to managing RCS pressure transients.

Section A states that the NuScale pressurizer and associated RCS components comply with ASME BPVC Class 1 requirements, while Section B describes the system features (such as degasification and pressure-retaining functions) that are designed to maintain RCS pressure boundary integrity, thus providing specific design details that support the general compliance claim in Section A.

Section A references the inspection, shipment, and installation requirements for the reactor pressure vessel, which is a component described in Section B as containing the integral pressurizer relevant to RCS pressure transient management.

Section A and Section B both discuss inspection and testing requirements for reactor vessel and RCS components, referencing ASME BPVC and related codes to ensure compliance with quality and safety standards relevant to pressure boundary integrity.

Section A outlines the inspection, shipment, and storage requirements for the reactor pressure vessel (RPV), which is a key component described in Section B as part of the reactor coolant system (RCS) and its pressure management features, thus providing structural and quality assurance context for the system described in Section B.

Section A discusses the challenges in detecting RCS boundary leakage, which is relevant to Section B's focus on compliance with overpressure protection codes, as undetected leakage could impact the adequacy of overpressure protection measures required by regulatory standards.

Section A describes the practical limitations of leakage detection in the NuScale RCS, while Section B asserts that the RCS components, including the pressurizer, are designed and constructed to meet regulatory codes (ASME BPVC and 10 CFR 50.55a), thus connecting operational detection challenges with the underlying code-compliant design basis.

Section A discusses the challenges in detecting RCS boundary leakage due to the NuScale design, while Section B outlines inspection and handling requirements for the reactor vessel, both addressing aspects of RCS integrity but without directly referencing or specifying each other.

Section A references the specific ASME BPVC code cases and regulatory provisions that are generally described as being met in Section B, thereby providing detailed provisions and compliance mechanisms for the general compliance statement in Section B.

Section A references the ASME BPVC Section III code cases for design and construction, which are also cited in Section B as the standards governing the shipment, handling, and storage of the reactor vessel, thus connecting both sections through their reliance on the same code requirements.

Section A establishes that the NuScale reactor pressure vessel and pressure-retaining components comply with ASME BPVC Section III, while Section B provides specific details on how the reactor vessel's shipment, installation, and environmental controls also adhere to ASME BPVC Section III and related standards, thus Section B specifies implementation details for the general compliance stated in Section A.

Section A provides a general overview of the RCS and mentions the integral pressurizer as a component, while Section B gives detailed design information about the RCS components, including the pressurizer, thus specifying the general description from Section A.

Section A provides a general overview of the RCS and its integral pressurizer, while Section B specifies inspection and testing requirements for RCS components, including those relevant to pressure management, thereby detailing how the system's adequacy is maintained.

Section A provides a general overview of the RCS and its components, including the integral pressurizer, while Section B specifies operational features and functions of the RCS and pressurizer relevant to pressure management.

Section A provides a general overview of the RCS and its reliance on natural circulation and inclusion of an integral pressurizer, while Section B specifies the detailed components and subsystems (including the pressurizer) that implement the design described in Section A.

Section A describes the overall design and components of the NuScale RCS, including the integral pressurizer, while Section B details the inspection and testing requirements for RCS components, which are necessary to ensure the adequacy and reliability of the design described in Section A.

Section A provides a general overview of the RCS and its components, including the integral pressurizer, while Section B specifies operational features and functions of the RCS and pressurizer relevant to pressure management.

Section A discusses the system evaluation criteria for optimizing NuScale Power Plant performance, which relies on the design details of RCS components—including the integral pressurizer—described in Section B.

Section A discusses the system evaluation criteria for optimal performance of the NuScale Power Plant, including the use of the DHRS for decay heat removal, while Section B details the inspection and classification requirements for DHRS components, connecting them through the operational reliability and regulatory compliance of the DHRS, which is integral to managing RCS pressure transients.

Section A discusses the evaluation of system performance criteria, including pressure parameters, while Section B describes specific design features of the RCS and pressurizer that enable pressure management, thus providing the technical basis for the evaluation in Section A.
